mappings.rationaleAI: The Reformation producing Lutheranism, Calvinism, Anglicanism, and Anabaptism directly addresses the Protestant Reformation (D.1). Catholic Counter-Reformation relates to C.2.c. The Swiss, French, and Low Countries reformations (D.1.c), English reformation (D.1.d), radical movements (D.1.f), and wars of religion (D.1.h) are all covered.
The Holy Roman Empire's Investiture Controversy and Reformation-era religious wars connect to the medieval Roman Catholic Church (C.1) and the Protestant Reformation's German context (D.1).

The Enlightenment's Deism and secularism as challenges to Protestant orthodoxy connect to the challenge of rationalism, Deism, and the Enlightenment (D.2.c). The broader impact on modern-age Protestantism relates to D.3.
Elizabeth I's Elizabethan Religious Settlement creating the Church of England as a moderate Protestant institution directly addresses the English reformation (D.1.d).

The Renaissance's recovery of classical antiquity and its relationship to Christianity connect to the medieval and Renaissance era of the Roman Catholic Church (C.1.a). Renaissance art's religious iconography relates to art and iconography (F.7).

The Byzantine Empire as continuation of Rome centered on Constantinople, with Orthodox Christianity and the Great Schism, directly addresses the growing division between Eastern and Western Christianity (A.3) and the history of Eastern Orthodoxy (B).

Charlemagne's enforcement of Christianization and promotion of the Carolingian Renaissance connect to developments affecting institutions and practices in the West and expansion of Christianity (A.3.c).

Napoleon's Concordat with the Catholic Church connects to the modern age of Roman Catholicism (C.4). His reshaping of European borders affected Protestant churches, relating to the modern age of Protestantism (D.3).

Luther's Ninety-five Theses, sola fide, sola scriptura, and vernacular Bible translation directly address Luther and the German Reformation (D.1.b). Lutheranism as a denomination relates to Lutheran churches (G.3.d.i).
The Ottoman Empire's millet system and rule over Orthodox Christian populations directly addresses Eastern Orthodoxy under the Ottomans (B.2.a).
The Spanish Empire's role in colonial missionary endeavors connects to the role of the church in the explorations and colonial policies (C.2.e).
Christianity as a comprehensive article covering doctrines, branches, and history addresses the entire scope of Section 827.

The Catholic Church with its hierarchical structure, apostolic succession, and Magisterium directly addresses the history of the Roman Catholic Church (C) and Roman Catholicism as a major traditional form (G.2).
Catherine the Great's reign over Russia connects to the Church of Russia during the 18th century (B.2.b).
The Crusades as papally authorized military expeditions connect to the development of the papacy as spiritual and temporal power in the medieval era (C.1.a).

Locke's advocacy for religious toleration and church-state separation connects to the challenge of rationalism and the Enlightenment to Protestantism (D.2.c) and developments in English Protestantism (D.2.d).
The Middle Ages covering Church and monasticism, Crusades, and scholasticism directly relates to the growing East-West division (A.3) and the medieval Roman Catholic Church (C.1).
Rome as headquarters of the Catholic Church and center of early Christianity connects to the establishment of Christianity as state religion (A.2.a) and Roman Catholicism (G.2).
Ancient Rome's transition from polytheistic religion to Christianity connects to the origins of the primitive church (A.1) and the early Christian Church under Constantine (A.2).
Protestantism with sola scriptura, sola fide, and diverse denominations directly addresses the history of Protestantism (D) and Protestantism as a major traditional form (G.3).
Aquinas's Summa Theologiae and Thomistic synthesis represent writings of post-patristic theologians (E.5), Christian philosophy (E.7), and Trinitarian doctrine (F.1.f).
Voltaire's polemics against Catholic Church dogma and advocacy for deism and religious toleration directly address the challenge of rationalism, Deism, and the Enlightenment (D.2.c).
Galileo's trial by the Roman Inquisition for championing heliocentrism exemplifies the relation of Christianity to culture and science (A.2.c).
The Age of Discovery's European maritime exploration and conquest of the Americas directly relates to missionary endeavours and the role of the church in colonial policies (C.2.e).

The Eastern Orthodox Church as a communion of autocephalous churches with the Great Schism of 1054 directly addresses the history of Eastern Orthodoxy (B) and Eastern Orthodoxy as a major traditional form (G.1).
Joan of Arc's canonization in 1920 connects to the saintly life (F.6.c). Her claimed divine visions relate to Christian mysticism (E.6).
Kant's philosophy of religion grounding morality connects to Christian philosophy (E.7).
Copernicus as a canon who formulated heliocentrism exemplifies the relationship between religious institutions and scientific change, relating to Christianity and culture (A.2.c).
